content.text: Environment config and secrets separation is the practice of keeping deployment-time configuration (model names, endpoint URLs, rate limits, log levels) and runtime credentials (API keys, tokens, database passwords) in entirely separate stores, injected by different mechanisms, with different access controls, and with different rotation policies. The underlying principle comes from the 12-factor app methodology, which states that configuration is everything likely to vary between deploys — and that configuration must never be committed to source control alongside the code that uses it.

For agentic AI services, the distinction between config and secrets is especially important because both categories contain values that look similar on the surface (strings injected at runtime) but carry very different risk profiles. An incorrect model name causes a failed API call. An exposed API key enables anyone who finds it to make arbitrary LLM API calls at your cost, extract data from your agent's tools, or impersonate your service with downstream systems.
The separation enforces a clear rule:
config files live in the repository; secrets never do

) are reviewed in pull requests, diffed in git history, and deployed with the application. Secrets are stored in a dedicated secret manager, injected at deploy time as environment variables or mounted files, and rotated on a schedule without any code change.

A committed secret is a permanent liability. Even after the secret is revoked and removed from the repository, it remains in the git history visible to anyone who clones the repo, including future contributors and any attacker who gains read access. Public repositories are routinely scanned by automated tools within seconds of a push; secrets committed to a public repo should be considered compromised immediately.

Beyond security, clear separation solves an operational problem. Without it, changing the model an agentic service uses in production requires either a code change (which triggers the full CI/CD pipeline) or a manual deployment intervention (which is error-prone and undocumented). When the model name lives in a config file, changing it is a one-line PR that goes through review, is traceable in git history, and can be rolled back with a revert.
Secrets never appear in git history.
The attack surface for credential theft through source code access is eliminated.
Config changes are reviewed and audited.
in the production config file is a visible, reviewable, rollback-able change.
API keys can be rotated on a 30-day schedule in the secret manager without touching the application code or config files.
The same application binary can be promoted from staging to production; only the config and injected secrets differ.
Schema validation catches config errors early.
Config files in the repo can be validated against a schema in CI, catching typos or missing required fields before the service reaches production.
The secret manager grants each environment's deployment role access only to the secrets it needs, so a compromised staging service cannot read production credentials.
A secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager) must be provisioned, IAM-configured, and maintained. This is non-trivial setup for a small team.
Developers need a local secret-fetching mechanism (a
file that is git-ignored, or a CLI tool that pulls from the vault) to run the service locally. Without a standard setup, developers may resort to hard-coding secrets.

If a secret is rotated in the manager but the running pod has not restarted, it continues using the old secret from its environment. Rotation must trigger either a rolling restart or a dynamic secret reload mechanism.
Operational complexity scales with the number of environments.
Each additional environment (feature env, performance test env, hotfix env) needs its own config file and its own set of secrets in the manager.
The separation operates at three layers. At the
, environment-specific YAML config files live in a
directory. They contain non-sensitive values: the LLM model ID, API endpoint base URLs, per-environment rate limits, log verbosity, feature flags, and timeout values. A
entry and a pre-commit hook prevent any file matching
or containing secret-shaped strings from being committed.
, API keys and tokens are stored encrypted. Each environment has a separate path in the vault (e.g.

) and a separate IAM role that can read only that path. Rotation is automated: the manager generates a new key, verifies it works, replaces the old value, and waits a grace period before revoking the old key.
, the CI/CD pipeline fetches secrets from the manager at deploy time and injects them as environment variables into the container. The application reads
at startup. Secrets are never written to disk and never logged. The Kubernetes deployment manifest references a

attrs.alt: Config and secrets separation: Git repository holds env-specific config files (safe to commit); Secret Manager holds API keys and tokens (encrypted, never in git); CI/CD pipeline fetches secrets and injects them. All three feed the Agentic Service Runtime separately per Dev, Staging, and Production environments.

content.text: Config flows left-to-right from the git repository into each environment unchanged. Secrets flow from the secret manager through the CI/CD pipeline (masked in logs) into the runtime. The agentic service sees both as environment variables at startup but they originate from entirely different sources with entirely different access controls.
Use strict separation from day one,
even on solo projects. The cost of setting up a
file and git-ignoring it is near zero; the cost of a leaked secret is high.
when the service is deployed to a shared environment (staging, production) accessible by more than one developer.
using a schema (Pydantic, JSON Schema, or cue) so that a missing required field is caught before the service reaches production.
The model name, timeout values, and log level are not secrets. Treating them as secrets forces unnecessary secret manager round-trips and obscures what is actually sensitive.
Do not store feature flags as secrets.
Feature flags belong in config files or a dedicated feature-flag service, not the secret manager, because they need to be readable and auditable without special access.
directory in the repo with one YAML file per environment:
file that lists every secret the service needs with placeholder values. Git-ignore
to prevent actual values from being committed.
from Yelp) that scans every staged file for secret-shaped patterns and blocks the commit if any are found.
Store all secrets in a managed secret store. For AWS, create a secret in AWS Secrets Manager at the path
. Attach an IAM policy that allows the service's execution role to

In CI/CD, use environment-scoped secrets (GitHub Actions Environments, GitLab Protected Variables) to inject secrets into the deployment job. The job fetches the secret, sets it as an environment variable, and passes it to the Kubernetes Secret manifest.
class (or equivalent) that reads config from the YAML file and secrets from environment variables. Validate at startup; fail fast with a clear error if a required value is missing.
Enable secret scanning in GitHub (Settings → Code security → Secret scanning) and on the main branch's push hook so that any accidentally committed secret triggers an immediate alert.

# config/config.prod.yaml — safe to commit, no secrets
llm:
  model: claude-opus-4-5
  max_tokens: 4096
  temperature: 0.0
  timeout_seconds: 30

api:
  base_url: https://api.anthropic.com
  rate_limit_rpm: 1000

tools:
  web_search:
    enabled: true
    max_results: 10
    timeout_seconds: 5
  code_sandbox:
    enabled: true
    runtime: python3.12

logging:
  level: INFO
  format: json

feature_flags:
  streaming_responses: true
  parallel_tool_calls: true
Pydantic Settings — Config + Secrets at Startup

# agent/settings.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings
from pydantic import Field
import yaml
from pathlib import Path

class LLMConfig(BaseSettings):
    """Non-sensitive LLM configuration loaded from YAML."""
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    temperature: float
    timeout_seconds: int

class AgentSettings(BaseSettings):
    """Runtime settings: config from YAML, secrets from environment."""

    # Secret: read from environment variable (never from file)
    anthropic_api_key: str = Field(..., env="ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
    database_url: str = Field(..., env="DATABASE_URL")

    # Config: populated below from the YAML file
    llm: LLMConfig

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, env: str = "prod") -> "AgentSettings":
        config_path = Path(f"config/config.{env}.yaml")
        raw = yaml.safe_load(config_path.read_text())
        # Merge YAML config with environment secrets
        return cls(
            llm=LLMConfig(**raw["llm"]),
            # anthropic_api_key and database_url are read from env automatically
        )

# Usage at startup:
# settings = AgentSettings.load(env=os.environ.get("APP_ENV", "prod"))
# agent = Agent(api_key=settings.anthropic_api_key, model=settings.llm.model)
GitHub Actions: Inject Secrets via Environment

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    environment: production          # scopes secrets to this environment
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy agent service
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
        run: |
          kubectl create secret generic agent-secrets \
            --from-literal=ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" \
            --from-literal=DATABASE_URL="$DATABASE_URL" \
            --namespace=prod \
            --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          # Config is a ConfigMap from the repo file (no secrets)
          kubectl create configmap agent-config \
            --from-file=config.yaml=config/config.prod.yaml \
            --namespace=prod \
            --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
HashiCorp Vault: Fetch Secret at Deploy Time

# Kubernetes deployment using Vault Agent sidecar injector
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: agent
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        # Vault agent sidecar annotation
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: "true"
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-api-key: "secret/prod/anthropic"
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-api-key: |
          {{- with secret "secret/prod/anthropic" -}}
          export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="{{ .Data.data.api_key }}"
          {{- end }}
        vault.hashicorp.com/role: "agent-prod"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: agent
          # Config from ConfigMap (safe, committed to repo)
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /app/config
      volumes:
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: agent-config
A team building a customer-support agent using an Anthropic LLM initially stored the API key in a
file that was git-ignored locally. When a developer provisioned a new server and cloned the repo, they added the key to a

file for convenience, then committed it. GitHub's secret scanning flagged the commit within 30 seconds and sent an alert, but the repo was briefly public during a repository transfer, and the key was scraped. The team revoked the key, rotated to a new one, and implemented AWS Secrets Manager for all environments. They also added
as a pre-commit hook so no secret could reach a commit regardless of file name, and added GitHub secret scanning with push protection enabled on all branches.
Config and secrets are like the

to a building. The blueprint (config) is a shared document: it is filed with the city, reviewed by architects and inspectors, and anyone with legitimate access to the project can read it. It tells you what the building is for, how big the rooms are, and what services are on which floor. The keys (secrets) are distributed on a strict need-to-know basis: only the people who must enter a room get a key, the keys are numbered and logged, and they are replaced on a schedule. You do not print the room key codes on the blueprint. The two artefacts have entirely different distribution, access, and lifecycle models — even though both are ultimately used to operate the same building.
The most common mistake is committing a
file that contains real secrets. Git-ignore patterns only work if the file was never previously tracked. If
was committed once and then removed, the secret remains in git history. Use
to rewrite history if this happens, then rotate the secret immediately.

Application startup logs that print all environment variables (a common debugging shortcut) will expose secrets in log aggregation systems. Never log environment variables wholesale. If a startup diagnostic is needed, log only the names of expected variables (not their values) and confirm each is present or absent.
Mixing Config and Secrets in the Same Object

Storing both the model name and the API key in the same Kubernetes Secret object means that every engineer with Secret read access can see the API key when they only needed to check the model name. Use a ConfigMap for config and a Secret for credentials, even if both are injected as environment variables.
Forgetting to Rotate After a Breach

When a secret is detected in a commit, teams sometimes revoke it and re-add it to the secret manager under the same name, then forget to check whether the old value appeared in any CI logs, build artefacts, or deployment scripts. A thorough rotation requires checking all downstream systems that may have cached the old value.
Install detect-secrets as a pre-commit hook.
to create an allowlist of known false positives, then enforce the hook in CI as well.
Enable push protection on all branches in GitHub.
This blocks a push containing a detected secret at the server side, providing a second line of defence after the pre-commit hook.
Use separate secret manager paths per environment.
A staging service's IAM role should have zero access to production paths, making privilege escalation from staging to production structurally impossible.
A Pydantic or JSON Schema validation step in the CI pipeline catches missing or mis-typed config values before they reach any environment.
AWS Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation via Lambda; Vault supports lease-based rotation. Never rely on manual rotation schedules — they slip.
Document the distinction clearly for new engineers.
Add a section to the repo README that explicitly lists what belongs in config files and what goes in the secret manager. Onboarding is the highest-risk moment for secret leaks.

Reading config from a YAML file at startup is essentially free. Secret manager round-trips add 50–200 ms per request at startup (not per agent call), which is acceptable. For high-frequency rotation or many secrets, cache the secret in memory at startup and refresh on a background schedule rather than fetching on every request.
Fetch from the secret manager once at process start and store in memory. Re-fetch on a background timer (e.g. every 10 minutes) to pick up rotated values without restarting the service.
For config values that change frequently (feature flags, rate limits), mount the config YAML as a Kubernetes ConfigMap and use inotify or a polling loop to reload the file without a restart.

AWS Secrets Manager has API rate limits. On systems with many pods starting simultaneously (e.g. during a rolling deploy), fetching the same secret from 50 pods at once can hit rate limits. Use the AWS SDK's built-in caching layer or a sidecar injector to retrieve the secret once per node.
Managed secret storage with built-in rotation, audit via CloudTrail, and fine-grained IAM policies. Integrates natively with ECS, EKS, and Lambda.

Self-hosted or Vault Cloud. Dynamic secrets (generates a temporary credential on each request), lease-based rotation, and a rich plugin ecosystem. The Vault Agent sidecar injects secrets directly into pod environment variables.
Azure Key Vault / GCP Secret Manager.
Cloud-native equivalents for Azure and GCP deployments. Both integrate with their respective Kubernetes managed services.
A pre-commit hook and CI scanner that identifies secret-shaped strings using entropy analysis and regex patterns. Supports a baseline file to manage false positives.
Reads settings from environment variables, dotenv files, and other sources with type validation. The standard approach for Python agentic services.
A Kubernetes operator that synchronises secrets from external providers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP) into native Kubernetes Secrets, keeping them refreshed automatically.

The primary security guarantee of this pattern is that a git repository breach does not expose operational credentials. An attacker who exfiltrates the entire repository gets model names, endpoint URLs, and feature flags — none of which are useful for impersonating the service or accessing customer data. The secrets required for that are in the secret manager, protected by IAM, and never stored in the repo.
AWS Secrets Manager and Vault support encrypting secrets with a customer-managed KMS key (CMK). A compromised storage backend does not expose the plaintext secret without also compromising the CMK.

Secret manager access logs (CloudTrail, Vault audit log) record every read, write, and rotation event with the IAM identity that triggered it. Enable these logs and alert on unexpected access patterns.

If a required environment variable is absent at startup, the service must refuse to start rather than falling back to a default. A silent fallback to an insecure default is worse than a deployment failure.
Config (model names, endpoints, rate limits) belongs in version-controlled YAML files — one per environment — reviewed in pull requests and traceable in git history.
Secrets (API keys, tokens, database passwords) belong in a secret manager, injected as environment variables at deploy time, and never in git under any circumstances.
Use a pre-commit hook (detect-secrets) and GitHub push protection to catch accidental secret commits before they reach the repository.
Validate config files against a schema in CI so that missing or mis-typed values fail the pipeline before reaching any environment.
Use separate IAM roles and secret manager paths per environment so that a compromised staging service cannot read production credentials.
Automate secret rotation in the secret manager; never rely on manual rotation schedules.
A service must fail fast at startup when a required secret is absent — never silently fall back to a default value.
